noun.title: Noun
noun.html: A word that names a person, place, thing, or idea. In Latin, nouns inflect for (six forms — nominative, genitive, dative, accusative, ablative, vocative) and (singular and plural). Every noun belongs to one of five and carries a fixed (masculine, feminine, or neuter), shown in the dictionary entry.

verb.title: Verb
verb.html: A word that names an action or a state. A Latin verb inflects for , , , , and . One verb has roughly 150 distinct forms; the four listed in the dictionary entry are enough to generate them all.

adjective.title: Adjective
adjective.html: A word that modifies a noun. Latin adjectives agree with the noun they modify in , , and — so an adjective has all three gender forms and shifts among them. The dictionary entry shows the three nominative-singular endings (e.g. novus, -a, -um).

pronoun.title: Pronoun
pronoun.html: A word that stands in for a noun (he, she, they, that one). Latin pronouns inflect for case, number, and gender like adjectives, often with irregular forms in some slots.

preposition.title: Preposition
preposition.html: An indeclinable word that introduces a noun phrase and specifies its grammatical relationship to the rest of the sentence (in urbe, “in the city”). Each preposition governs a specific — usually accusative or ablative; the dictionary entry says which.

conjunction.title: Conjunction
conjunction.html: An indeclinable word that joins clauses or phrases (et, “and”; sed, “but”; quod, “because”). Latin distinguishes coordinating (et) from subordinating (ut, cum) conjunctions.

enclitic.title: Enclitic
enclitic.html: A particle attached to the end of the preceding word rather than written separately (-que = “and”; -ne = question marker; -ve = “or”). Primaque in dictionary order is prima + -que.

nominative.title: Nominative
nominative.html: The case of the grammatical subject and of predicate nouns/adjectives (puella ridet, “the girl smiles”). It’s also the dictionary-entry form of a Latin noun.

genitive.title: Genitive
genitive.html: The case of possession and origin (puellae liber, “the girl’s book”), and of dependence on certain nouns and verbs. The genitive singular is the second principal part of a noun’s dictionary entry — it shows which the noun belongs to.

dative.title: Dative
dative.html: The case of the indirect object and the recipient (puellae librum dedit, “he gave a book to the girl”). Also used with certain adjectives (“dear to her”) and verbs (“trust in him”).

accusative.title: Accusative
accusative.html: The case of the direct object (puellam amat, “he loves the girl”), and the case governed by many prepositions of motion-toward (in urbem, “into the city”). For neuter nouns and adjectives, the accusative form always equals the nominative — both singular and plural.

ablative.title: Ablative
ablative.html: A case of separation, means, manner, time, and place (gladio interfecit, “killed with a sword”). Also the case governed by many prepositions of rest-in and motion-from (in urbe, “in the city”; ex urbe, “out of the city”). Has no direct English equivalent.

vocative.title: Vocative
vocative.html: The case of direct address (Brute!, “Brutus!”). For most nouns the vocative is identical to the nominative; second-declension -us nouns have a distinct -e vocative.

singular.title: Singular
singular.html: A grammatical number used when one of something is meant.

plural.title: Plural
plural.html: A grammatical number used when more than one of something is meant. Latin has no dual number; one or many is the only distinction.

masculine.title: Masculine
masculine.html: One of the three Latin grammatical . Lexical gender — most nouns are assigned a gender that’s just a fact about them; it doesn’t always track sex (nauta, “sailor”, is masculine but ends like a feminine).

feminine.title: Feminine
feminine.html: One of the three Latin grammatical . Like masculine, lexical and largely arbitrary.

neuter.title: Neuter
neuter.html: One of the three Latin grammatical . Neuter nouns and adjectives always have the same form in the nominative and accusative (whether singular or plural); the plural nominative/accusative always ends in -a.

1st-person.title: 1st-person
1st-person.html: Refers to the speaker — I in the singular, we in the plural. In Latin the person is shown by the verb ending; pronouns (ego, nos) appear only when emphatic or contrastive.

2nd-person.title: 2nd-person
2nd-person.html: Refers to the addressee — you in the singular, you (plural) in the plural. Latin has distinct singular and plural pronouns (tu, vos); the verb ending also distinguishes them.

3rd-person.title: 3rd-person
3rd-person.html: Refers to a non-speaker, non-addressee — he, she, it, they. There’s no Latin pronoun for “it” as such; is, ea, id or demonstrative ille, illa, illud fill the role.

present.title: Present
present.html: The tense of action happening now or generally true (amat, “he loves” / “he is loving”). Built on the present stem (the first principal part).

imperfect.title: Imperfect
imperfect.html: The past tense of ongoing or repeated action (amabat, “he was loving” / “he used to love”). Built on the present stem with -ba- infix.

perfect.title: Perfect
perfect.html: The tense of completed action (amavit, “he loved” / “he has loved”). Built on the perfect stem (the third ).

pluperfect.title: Pluperfect
pluperfect.html: The past tense of action completed before another past action (amaverat, “he had loved”). Built on the perfect stem with -era- infix.

future.title: Future
future.html: The tense of action yet to happen (amabit, “he will love”). Different conjugations form the future with different endings.

indicative.title: Indicative
indicative.html: The mood of statement and question — the default mood for asserting that something is, was, or will be the case (amat, “he loves”).

subjunctive.title: Subjunctive
subjunctive.html: The mood of possibility, wish, command in subordinate clauses, hypothetical condition, and other non-assertive uses (amet, “may he love” / “let him love”). One of the most flexible features of Latin.

imperative.title: Imperative
imperative.html: The mood of direct command (ama!, “love!”). Latin has 2nd-person singular and plural imperatives (and rare 3rd-person forms); the singular often equals the present stem with no ending.

active.title: Active
active.html: The voice in which the subject is the agent of the action (puer librum legit, “the boy reads the book”).

passive.title: Passive
passive.html: The voice in which the subject undergoes the action (liber a puero legitur, “the book is read by the boy”). Latin has distinct passive endings for the present system; the perfect passive is built from the + a form of esse.

infinitive.title: Infinitive
infinitive.html: A non-finite verb form that names the action itself (“to love”, “to be loved”). Latin has six infinitives — present/perfect/future × active/passive — though the present active is the most common. The present active infinitive is the of a verb’s dictionary entry.

perfect-passive-participle.title: Perfect passive participle
perfect-passive-participle.html: The verbal adjective formed from the perfect stem of a verb’s passive voice (“having been loved”). It declines like a first/second declension adjective (so it agrees with its noun in case, number, and gender) and appears throughout the passive system, in deponent verbs, and in ablative absolute constructions. The fourth of a verb gives this stem (in the neuter form, amatum).

supine.title: Supine
supine.html: A defective verbal noun in -um (or -u), used in a narrow set of constructions: with verbs of motion to express purpose (venit spectatum, “he came to see”), and as the of a verb’s dictionary entry — the form that gives the stem for the and the future active participle.

gender.title: Gender
gender.html: The three Latin grammatical genders are , , and . Nouns have a fixed gender; adjectives shift to match the noun they modify.

declension.title: Declension
declension.html: One of five patterns of noun (and adjective) inflection in Latin, identified by the genitive singular ending. 1st declension: -ae (feminine, like forma). 2nd: -i (masculine and neuter). 3rd: -is (all three genders). 4th: -us (mostly masculine). 5th: -ei (mostly feminine).

principal-parts.title: Principal parts
principal-parts.html: The set of four verb forms a dictionary entry gives so the reader can build any inflected form. For a regular verb: = the lemma’s dictionary form (1st-person singular present indicative active, e.g. amo); = present active infinitive (amare); = 1st-person singular perfect indicative active (amavi); = supine (amatum).

first-principal-part.title: First principal part
first-principal-part.html: The first slot in a verb’s dictionary entry — the 1st-person singular present indicative active (e.g. amo, “I love”). This is the form the lemma is filed under, and it gives the present stem from which all present-system forms are built.

second-principal-part.title: Second principal part
second-principal-part.html: The second slot in a verb’s dictionary entry — the present active (e.g. amare, “to love”). It shows which the verb belongs to and how the present system inflects.

third-principal-part.title: Third principal part
third-principal-part.html: The third slot in a verb’s dictionary entry — the 1st-person singular perfect indicative active (e.g. amavi, “I have loved”). It gives the perfect stem, from which all forms in the , , and future-perfect active are built.

fourth-principal-part.title: Fourth principal part
fourth-principal-part.html: The fourth slot in a verb’s dictionary entry — the (e.g. amatum). It gives the stem for the and the future active participle.
